data.title: Trump and Xi set to discuss TikTok, Nvidia, trade, and more
data.description:
Expectations are high for a phone call between President Trump and President Xi Jinping at 9 AM ET, as a thawing of U.S.-China tensions is said to be in the making. In recent weeks, negotiations between the two sides have taken place at a high level, including meetings between Secretary of State Marco Rubio, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, and Secretary of War Pete Hegseth with their Chinese counterparts. A lot is on the agenda, and if everything goes to schedule, a trip by Trump to China could happen soon, marking the first American president to visit the country since 2017.

First on the agenda: There might finally be a deal for TikTok. The app, owned by Chinese parent ByteDance (BDNCE), has survived in its current form after years of accusations that its algorithm was a national security threat due to mass surveillance or propaganda manipulation. Keep an eye on the structure of any spinoff, and on whether the app will end up in the hands of "all American investors" or contain some level of Chinese involvement.

Washington and Beijing also agreed to extend their trade truce for another 90 days in early August after a tit-for-tat tariff battle spiraled out of control. Points of tension still center around the export of fentanyl-related chemicals, China's relationship with Russia, and purchases of U.S. farm products like soybeans. "We're pretty close to a deal," Trump said this week during a press conference. "We may do an extension with China, but it's an extension based on the same terms that we have right now, which are pretty good terms.”

Other items: Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) is on the list after being hit with an antitrust investigation by Beijing, and to finally resolve whether (and how) the AI juggernaut could sell its chips to China. The U.S. federal government was set to receive a portion of revenue on those sales, in what some have called a "reverse tariff," but clarity is needed on business there. Potential aircraft orders from Boeing (BA), as well as jet engine components from suppliers like GE Aerospace (GE), Honeywell (HON), and Collins Aerospace (RTX), will likely be discussed, as well as key rare earth elements that have played an outsized role in the U.S.-China trade war.

data.title: One year in, Alaska Air's deal for Hawaiian Airlines is paying off
data.description:
The closing of the merger between Alaska Airlines (NYSE:ALK) and Hawaiian Airlines happened one year ago. Alaska Air Group (NYSE:ALK) acquired Hawaiian Airlines for $1.9 billion in cash plus about $900 million in assumed debt. Both airlines continue to operate under their respective brands but now fall under the same corporate umbrella. The combined entity serves over 140 destinations and provides expanded connectivity through the Oneworld Alliance, offering broader international reach and greater loyalty program integration.

During Alaska Air's (NYSE:ALK) last earnings conference call, management noted that the powerful combination of the two networks is already delivering greater utility and choice for Hawaii residents and visitors alike. The changes were noted to have driven a quarterly profit for the airline company's Hawaiian assets for the first time since 2019. During Q2, Alaska Air's (ALK) premium revenue was up 5% year-over-year, driven higher by the new Hawaiian assets. The expectation is that the Hawaiian assets will continue to boost overall profits for Alaska Air (ALK) as more synergies are unlocked to boost margins.

Looking at the broad U.S. airline sector, the Alaska Air-Hawaiian Airlines deal created a significant challenger to Delta Airlines (DAL), United Airlines (UAL), American Airlines (AAL), and Southwest Airlines (LUV) on the West Coast by linking Hawaii to more mainland routes. While Alaska Air's (ALK) market share remains far smaller than the Big Four airlines, the combination is expected to drive more robust competition and may affect fare structures, route availability, and service quality.

data.title: Elon Musk focused on xAI after leaving Washington; Grok sees 64M monthly users: report
data.description:
Elon Musk laid out his vision for his AI startup xAI in a company-wide meeting where he told employees that he wanted to build systems that were “maximally truth-seeking” while also previewing plans to build a Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) competitor called Macrohard, The New York Times reported.

“We are the only company where the mission is truth,” Musk told his workers as part of an hour-and-a-half presentation that was listened to by The New York Times. “If you force the A.I. to lie or believe things that are not true, you’re at great risk of creating a dystopian future,” the report added.

The AI company's chatbot Grok has reached 64 million monthly users, the report noted, citing a company leader.

xAI did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Since his falling out with President Donald Trump in June, the Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) CEO has redoubled his efforts with xAI, which was recently valued at $120B.

Musk has deemed government a hopeless problem, but he believes A.I. will revolutionize society. And he insists xAI can build technology that will eventually help his other businesses, like Tesla and SpaceX, the report added.

Over the summer, Musk spent most of his time at xAI’s offices in Palo Alto, California, working in all-day spurts that sometimes stretched into the next day, NYT added, citing people familiar with the company’s operations. Occasionally, as he has with his other companies over the years, Musk has slept at the office.

Musk’s focus on xAI raises questions about how much time he giving to his other companies, and comes as Tesla’s board of directors has been pushing to give him a trillion-dollar pay package, which they say will motivate him to improve the company's performance, the report added. On Monday, Musk posted on X, “Daddy is very much home,” and detailed his schedule, which included 12 hours of meetings at Tesla and a visit to xAI’s data center.

Despite Musk’s focus on xAI, the company's had a tough summer, the report added, citing interviews with 12 people with knowledge of the company’s operations.

Musk has revamped xAI on the fly. He has led an aggressive recruiting drive for engineers. And he has pushed out several prominent researchers, even as others have left because they thought xAI had abandoned science in favor of attention-grabbing products, like a chatbot which sometimes produced offensive material and flirty A.I. chat companions, the report added.

Simultaneously,  xAI has is spending billions of dollars on technology while it is not known how much money it is generating. Musk and his executives said on Wednesday that Grok now has 64 million monthly users. ChatGPT gets about 700 million weekly users for OpenAI, the report noted

In addition, Musk has made predictions that xAI's product would help quintuple advertising revenue to $10 billion a year at X, formerly Twitter, the report added.

In the meeting, Musk reiterated his commitment to creating a competitor to Microsoft, calling it “Macrohard” that would use Grok to create software as well as an A.I. product for children ages 2 to 12 called “Baby Grok,” the report noted.

Musk and his employees also noted the importance of xAI’s connections to Musk's other companies, stating that Grok was already powering the voice of Tesla’s Optimus humanoid robots, which are being developed to handle work inside factories and other businesses, according to the report.

Recently, Musk has publicly supported a Tesla shareholder proposal calling on the company to invest in xAI. Shareholders are slated to vote on the proposal in early November.

"This shareholder vote decides the future of Tesla and may affect the future of the world," Musk said in a post on X on Thursday.

In recent months, several executives at xAI had left after clashing with two of Musk’s closest advisers over concerns about the AI startup's management and financial health, the Wall Street Journal reported on Thursday.

data.title: 22nd Century shares rise as company announces debt-free balance sheet and new growth capital
data.description:
22nd Century Group (NASDAQ:XXII) on Friday announced that, following the closing of its Series A convertible preferred stock offering, it has repaid the remaining $3.9 million of its senior secured debt in full, sending shares up 23% in premarket trading.

Completion of the Series A convertible preferred stock offering and senior secured debt repayment resulted in an approximate $9.1 million increase in as-adjusted pro forma net tangible book value, or approximately $1.05 per share.

Management has plans to deploy a portion of capital to expand its very low nicotine tobacco leaf inventory in the fourth quarter of 2025, with inventory reserves allowing for production of more than one million cartons of VLN combustible products.

data.title: Scholastic falls after missing estimates, hit by education funding delays
data.description:
Scholastic (NASDAQ:SCHL) shares fell 13% in premarket trading on Friday after the company missed quarterly profit and revenue estimates amid a wider challenging environment.

Its education division faced sales pressure due to a volatile funding environment, including delayed and canceled federal education grants.

While ongoing tariff expenses were estimated at $10 million for the fiscal year, management indicated active monitoring of trade policy developments.

"Further, several states are facing budget impasses. In this challenging environment, we continue taking steps to strengthen this business for the long term," CEO Peter Warwick said. 

Scholastic (NASDAQ:SCHL) began fiscal 2026 with continued progress on cost discipline, IP-driven digital expansion, and real estate monetization initiatives, while affirming its full-year targets for revenue growth, profitability, and free cash flow. 

On guidance sensitivity, CFO Haji Glover highlighted potential retail market variability and tariff costs: "...the most important thing for us is the concerns of tariffs, as it reflects our business because we are a retail business. And those expenses, which we've already planned for, which is about $10 million this year."

Uncertainty in retail and school spending, as well as the pace of digital revenue recognition, were noted as potential variables impacting guidance.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings, this company has a Hold rating with a score of 2.71 out of 5.

data.title: Nvidia plans $500 million investment in UK's self-driving startup Wayve
FDA nicotine pouch pilot to ease manufacturers' research burden - report
data.description:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration will ease requirements on nicotine pouch manufacturers under a new pilot program, Reuters reported on Friday, citing internal meeting transcripts.

The FDA looks to ensure products offer a net benefit to public health by helping smokers switch away from cigarettes without creating new issues, such as a surge in youth use.

Under the pilot, however, the FDA will drop a requirement that manufacturers submit product-specific studies in several areas, the report said. 

The pilot initiative directly impacts major tobacco companies Philip Morris International (NYSE:PM) with its Zyn Ultra brand, Altria (NYSE:MO) with its on! and on! Plus brands, British American Tobacco (BTI) with its Velo mini brand, and Turning Point Brands (NYSE:TPB) with its Fre and Alp brands.

The FDA on Thursday  said that the pilot program it launched this month aims to increase efficiency and streamline the review process for premarket tobacco product applications (PMTAs) for nicotine pouch products.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: XXII, FDX, UPS, and more
data.description:
Stock futures inched up Friday morning, extending record-setting gains from the prior session as investors absorbed the Federal Reserve’s rate cut.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • 22nd Century Group (NASDAQ:XXII) +31% - Shares surged after the company announced it is now debt-free following repayment of $3.9M in senior secured debt. The move eliminates cash interest and amortization expense, lowering ongoing costs. Backed by new growth capital from its Series A preferred offering, XXII plans to expand its very low nicotine (VLN) tobacco leaf inventory, supporting production of 1M+ cartons of VLN products.
  • FedEx (NYSE:FDX) +5% - Shares climbed after the company beat FQ1 earnings estimates. For FY2026, FedEx projects 4%–6% revenue growth and EPS of $17.20–$19.00 (midpoint $18.10) vs. $18.36 consensus. “Our strategic initiatives, paired with our operational data platform moving 17M packages daily, position us to serve customers in any environment and drive long-term value,” said CEO Raj Subramaniam.
  • United Parcel Service (NYSE:UPS) +2% - Stock moved up, in tandem with FedEx's better-than-expected earnings report for the quarter, which lifted sentiment across the logistics sector. Separately, UPS confirmed it has scrapped its planned acquisition of Mexican courier Estafeta, stating that unmet closing conditions could not be resolved.

Biggest stock losers

  • Scholastic Corporation (NASDAQ:SCHL) -13% - Shares tumbled after missing FQ1 expectations, with Education Solutions revenue down 28% and Entertainment down 18%. Despite the miss, the company reaffirmed FY2026 guidance as adjusted EBITDA loss improved 8% to $55.7M, helped by cost cuts and stronger Children’s Publishing sales.
  • Lennar (NYSE:LEN) -3% - Shares of the home construction company slipped after Q3 results missed on both revenue and EPS. Net earnings fell ~50% Y/Y, with home sales revenue down 9% and gross margin contracting to 17.5% from 22.5% a year ago. For Q4, Lennar guided to 20K–21K new orders at an average sales price of $380K–$390K.

data.title: House Democrats call on FCC's Brendan Carr to resign over Kimmel suspension
data.description:
Top Democratic lawmakers have called for the resignation of Federal Communications Commission Chair Brendan Carr, following Disney (NYSE:DIS) unit ABC's decision to stop airing Jimmy Kimmel Live! indefinitely.

The late-night show was pulled off the air due to backlash over its host Kimmel's controversial comments about the killing of right-wing political activist Charlie Kirk.

The show's suspension came after Carr said on a podcast that ABC-affiliated stations "can find ways to change conduct and take action on Kimmel, or there's going to be additional work for the FCC ahead."

Carr "engaged in the corrupt abuse of power," House Democratic leaders said in a joint statement calling for his resignation. "He has disgraced the office he holds by bullying ABC, the employer of Jimmy Kimmel, and forcing the company to bend the knee to the Trump administration."

The statement came from House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-NY), Katherine Clark (D-MA), Pete Aguilar (D-CA), Ted Lieu (D-CA), Joe Neguse (D-CO), and Suzan DelBene (D-WA).

President Donald Trump on Thursday said local broadcast networks give him "totally bad publicity" and "maybe their license should be taken away," adding that the decision would be up to Carr.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
